However, I can provide information on Thymus, a genus of plants that includes Thymus serpyllum (wild thyme) and Thymus vulgaris (common thyme), both of which have similar properties and uses.

Here's a paragraph about Thymus (assuming Thymus verecundus refers to a member of this genus):

Thymus is a genus of flowering plants in the mint family, characterized by small, fragrant flowers and small, oval-shaped leaves. The health benefits of Thymus species include antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties, which can help to boost the immune system, reduce the severity of respiratory infections, and protect against cell damage. The bioactive constituents of Thymus include volatile oils (such as thymol and carvacrol), flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Thymus include teas, essential oils, and capsules, which are often used to support respiratory health and as a natural remedy for digestive issues.
